__Forward           __

For those who havenΓÇÖt had the chance to sit across from Dr\. Wickersham in his clinic, this book offers the next best thing\. Through its pages, his compassion, clarity, and transformative approach to pain come alive\. He teaches that pain deserves

compassionΓÇöboth from within and from those who care for us\. This book is a powerful

guide to understanding where pain comes from, how the body perceives it, andΓÇömost

importantlyΓÇöthat you deserve to be treated with respect, dignity, and integrity\.

Having Dr\. Wickersham as my doctor changed my life\. Reading his book may very well

do the same for you\. At the very least, you will gain a greater understanding of your

body and its response to pain\.

At the heart of my journey with chronic pain stands someone who didnΓÇÖt just treat my

conditionΓÇöhe saw me\. Dr\. Wickersham was the first to recognize me as more than a

person with an autoimmune disease\. In a world where chronic illness often makes you

feel like a list of symptoms or a case to be solved, being seen as a whole person

changed everything\.

Dr\. Wickersham helped shift my focus from what couldnΓÇÖt be fixed toward what could be

changedΓÇöreclaiming the parts of my life that still held meaning, joy, and purpose\. He

taught me that life isnΓÇÖt just about avoiding pain; itΓÇÖs about recognizing how to live well

despite having pain\. That single shift in mindsetΓÇöfrom ΓÇ£fix it or failΓÇ¥ to ΓÇ£live it and

adaptΓÇ¥ΓÇöhas been transformative\.

Living with Adult\-Onset StillΓÇÖs Disease, and chronic illness in general, means living with

deep unpredictability\. You donΓÇÖt just deal with the pain itself; you deal with fatigue, flares,

medication side effects, infections, and countless other things you never expected\.

Sometimes, what you fear most never happens\. Other times, the things you never

imagined would happen, do\. The ground shifts constantly beneath your feet\.

In the face of that vulnerability, itΓÇÖs easy to feel you have no control\. The truth is,

sometimes we donΓÇÖtΓÇöand thatΓÇÖs okay\. What matters is learning to embrace what we can

control: our mindset, our actions, and our willingness to adapt\. I often say, ΓÇ£The only

thing I can control is how I approach things\.ΓÇ¥ That one truth has grounded me in the

midst of the unknown\.

With this mindset, I now live by a personal risk\-versus\-benefit philosophy\. IΓÇÖve learned to

ask myself:

Is this worth it? Sometimes, the answer is a beautiful yesΓÇölike being present for the

birth of a grandchild, even if I know IΓÇÖll pay for it later in pain\. Other times, I walk away

from stress or conflict that offers nothing in return but suffering\. That discernment gives

me agency and peace\.

IΓÇÖve also discovered the quiet superpower of humor\. Laughter may not be a cure, but itΓÇÖs

an anchor\. Sometimes, you canΓÇÖt change the pain, but you can learn to bob and weave

around it\. You learn to be flexible, to adapt, and to fill the unchangeable with something

other than despair\. ItΓÇÖs not easy, but itΓÇÖs possible\. And when you get it right, even just for

a moment, itΓÇÖs beautiful\.

This journey has taught me that resilience isnΓÇÖt about being unbreakableΓÇöitΓÇÖs about

being creative, compassionate with yourself, and intentional about what matters\.

Chronic illness may be part of my life, but it doesnΓÇÖt get to write my whole story\. 

With support, perspective, and the right care, IΓÇÖve learned that my life is still mine to shape\.

Perhaps the greatest gift IΓÇÖve received is the reminder that we are all resilient\. Our bodies, though challenged, are incredible at adapting, and we, as people, are stronger than we realize\. Dr\. Wickersham helped me see that\. No matter what happens, we are capable of facing it\. We are capable of growing through it\. Even in pain, we are still powerful\.

To anyone walking a similar path: know that you are not powerless\. You are not your

illness\. You get to decide what you carry, what you let go, and what you prioritize\. When

you live from that truth, you take back your power and begin to write a new chapterΓÇönot

one that is pain\-free, but one that is full of purpose\.

Pain may be a part of our lives, but so are choice, meaning, and joy\. Thanks to people

like Dr\. Wickersham who remind us to focus on what truly matters, IΓÇÖve learned that

living fully isnΓÇÖt about what you loseΓÇöitΓÇÖs about what you choose\.

I wish every reader a deeper understanding of their body and mind, and the discovery of

resilience as a powerful asset to any treatment plan\.

__AuthorΓÇÖs Introduction__

__Preface to the 2025 Edition __

It has been over a decade since *Managing Life with Chronic Pain* was first published\. In that time, as a physician specializing in rheumatology and as someone who continues to live with __ankylosing spondylitis \(AS\)__, I have witnessed and experienced firsthand the remarkable evolution in our understanding of and approach to chronic pain\. The core message of that initial work that people can actively manage their lives despite pain remains as true as ever\. However, the tools, knowledge, and strategies available to us have expanded in ways that offer even greater hope and potential for improved well\-being\.

The impetus for this expanded edition, *The Resilient Path*, comes from a desire to integrate these significant advancements into the practical framework I originally described\. Pain science has evolved significantly\. We now speak with greater clarity about concepts like __central sensitization__, the role of __neuroinflammation__, and the distinct entity of __nociplastic pain__, terms that were less widely understood in 2011\. These insights have profound implications for how we approach treatment, moving us further toward personalized care\.

Similarly, the therapeutic landscape has broadened\. While prudent use of medication remains a cornerstone, there is now an even stronger emphasis on and evidence base for nonpharmacological interventions\. __Pain neuroscience education__ __\(PNE\)__ empowers patients by helping them understand *why* they hurt\. Psychological approaches like __acceptance and commitment therapy \(ACT\)__ help people build rich, meaningful lives even when pain persists\. The science behind exercise, nutrition, and sleep in modulating pain has grown significantly, offering actionable lifestyle strategies\. Our approach to existing treatments, such as opioid therapy, has become far more nuanced and cautious, informed by the hard lessons of the intervening years\.

This updated volume seeks to incorporate important new information into the original concept of the initial edition\. My goal is to augment and enrich timeless advice on building strong patient\-doctor relationships, fostering optimism, and finding personal meaning with the best of modern research\. The spirit of the original chapters is preserved, but their scope and depth have been considerably enhanced\. New chapters have also been added to address crucial topics that have gained prominence\.

Parts 1 and 2 focus on the science of chronic pain\. Some of the information may seem a little too academic and some of the explanations brief\. Do not worry, however; I wonΓÇÖt leave you confused or wondering too long\. These parts simply serve as a basis for the rest of the book, where many of the concepts introduced will be explained in more detail\.

Parts 3 through 5 focus more on practical tips for living with pain\. If sections 1 and 2 are too dense and not practical enough for you, feel free to skip to parts 3 through 5 and use the initial sections more as a reference\. 

My own journey with chronic pain continues, a constant reminder of the daily challenges and triumphs involved\. My struggle reinforces my belief in the power of informed self\-management and the resilience of the human spirit\. I offer this book in the hope that it will serve as an even more comprehensive and empowering guide for those navigating living with chronic pain, helping them find their own resilient path toward a better quality of life\.

Pendleton B\. Wickersham, MD

May 2025

__Introduction __

As a rheumatologist, I see patients struggling with chronic pain every day\. In fact, almost all my patients hurt to some extent\. I have now seen many different strategies for coping with the painΓÇösome that work and some that donΓÇÖt\. In society as a whole, chronic pain disrupts the daily lives of millions of people\. There is increased recognition of the problem, and more treatments are available now than ever before\. Taking pain medication is no longer considered a weakness\. Despite better understanding and more aggressive treatment of pain, though, many still struggle to function\.

I also have AS, a chronic inflammatory disease of the spine\. Because of the spine problems associated with this disease, I have had chronic pain for years\. After years of milder symptoms, I developed severe pain while in medical school\. In fact, I showed up for my rheumatology rotation one morning barely able to walk\. The rheumatologist teaching me at the time prescribed me a powerful muscle relaxer, which I took later that day\. I was two hours late the next morning \(and still somewhat sedated from the medication effects\)\. Because of my disease, I have had a chance to try for myself many of the strategies in this book\. I have experienced unpleasant side effects, such as the sedation I just mentioned, as well as good results from other treatments\.

What follows is an attempt to illustrate some of the most effective ways of dealing with chronic pain from the perspective of both a physician and a patient\. I recommend that you read through each section and figure out what ideas could work best for you\. Do not try them all at once; this book should be used as a guide offering many possible options for managing pain and living a better life\.

__Part 1: Understanding the Causes and Types of Pain__

__Chapter 1: The Modern Understanding of Pain: More Than a Symptom__ 

For centuries, pain was largely viewed through a relatively simple lens: a direct, almost mechanical consequence of injury or disease\. If a part of your body was damaged, it sent a signal to your brain, and you felt pain\. Treat the damage, and the pain would naturally resolve\. While this model holds true for many instances of __acute pain__ \(the kind you experience when you stub your toe or touch a hot stove\), it falls woefully short of explaining the persistent, often baffling nature of chronic pain\. As IΓÇÖve seen countless times in my rheumatology practice and experienced personally through living with AS, chronic pain is a far more complex adversary, one that often seems to take on a life of its own independent of any initial injury\.

The good news is that our understanding of pain and particularly of chronic pain has undergone a profound expansion in recent decades, especially since the original publication of this guide in 2011\. Understanding has moved beyond seeing pain as merely a symptom to recognizing it as a multifaceted experience that can itself become a disease process\. This shift in perspective is crucial because it opens new avenues for management and offers a more nuanced and validating framework for those who live with pain daily\.

__The Evolving Definition of Pain__

A cornerstone of this modern understanding is the updated definition of pain put forth by the __International Association for the Study of Pain \(IASP\)__ in 2020\. After years of deliberation by a multinational task force, they revised their long\-standing 1979 definition to:

*ΓÇ£An unpleasant sensory and emotional experience associated with, or resembling that associated with, actual or potential tissue damage\.ΓÇ¥*

This definition is accompanied by several key notes that further illuminate its depth: 

- *Pain is always a personal experience that is influenced to varying degrees by biological, psychological, and social factors\.*
- *Pain and nociception are different phenomena\. Pain cannot be inferred solely from activity in sensory neurons\.*
- *Through their life experiences, individuals learn the concept of pain\.*
- *A personΓÇÖs report of an experience as pain should be respected\.*
- *Although pain usually serves an adaptive role, it may have adverse effects on function and social and psychological well\-being\.*
- *Verbal description is only one of several behaviors to express pain; inability to communicate does not negate the possibility that a human or a nonhuman animal experiences pain\.*

What does this definition mean for you, the person living with pain? First, it validates that your pain is real, even if objective medical tests donΓÇÖt always pinpoint a clear structural cause\. The emphasis on pain as an ΓÇ£emotional experienceΓÇ¥ acknowledges the inherent suffering involved, moving beyond a purely physical interpretation\. The note clarifying that pain is not synonymous with nociception \(the neurological process of encoding and processing harmful or potentially harmful stimuli by the sensory nervous system\) is particularly important for chronic pain, as it helps explain why pain can persist long after tissues have healed or even arise without any identifiable tissue damage\. Your brain and nervous system are key players and not just passive recipients of signals\.

__The Biopsychosocial Model: A Holistic Framework__

This updated definition aligns perfectly with the __biopsychosocial model__ of pain, a framework that has become the bedrock of comprehensive pain care\. First proposed by Dr\. George Engel in 1977 as a broader model for illness, its application to chronic pain has been transformative\. Instead of focusing solely on the ΓÇ£bioΓÇ¥ \(the biological or physical aspectsΓÇölike tissue injury, __inflammation__, or nerve damage\), this model integrates the ΓÇ£psychoΓÇ¥ \(psychological factorsΓÇöthoughts, emotions, beliefs, coping strategies, __stress__, trauma history\) and the ΓÇ£socialΓÇ¥ \(social factorsΓÇöfamily support, work environment, socioeconomic status, cultural influences, access to care\)\.

Each of these factors plays an important role in chronic pain:

- __Biological factors__ might include ongoing inflammation in someone with __rheumatoid arthritis \(RA\)__, nerve damage from diabetes in someone else, or the pain from an arthritic knee in someone with an old injury\. Research continues to uncover new biological contributors, such as genetic predisposition, the role of the immune system \(including specialized brain immune cells called __glial cells__\), and even the influence of our gut bacteria \(the microbiome\)\.
- <a id="_fr6t3jjfgxm7"></a>__Psychological factors__ are equally critical\. For example, fear of movement \(__kinesiophobia__\) can lead to deconditioning and more pain\. Catastrophizing \(expecting the worst\) can amplify pain perception\. Conversely, positive coping strategies, __mindfulness__, and a sense of __self\-efficacy__ can significantly dampen the pain experience\. __Depression__ and __anxiety__, so common in chronic pain, are not just *reactions* to pain; they share underlying neurobiological pathways and can directly worsen pain intensity and impact\.
- <a id="_yephnsamg2ew"></a>__Social factors__ shape how we experience and cope with pain\. A supportive family and understanding employer can make a world of difference\. Conversely, social isolation, financial stress due to the inability to work, or feeling disbelieved by loved ones or healthcare providers can exacerbate suffering\. Cultural norms around expressing pain also play a role\.

Understanding pain through the biopsychosocial lens is empowering because it reveals multiple points of intervention\. If pain were purely biological, our options would be limited to physical treatments like medication or surgery\. But by acknowledging the psychological and social dimensions, we open the door to a much wider array of effective strategies, including psychological therapies, lifestyle modifications, stress management techniques, and building stronger social support networksΓÇöall of which we will explore in depth in this book\.

__Acute vs\. Chronic Pain: Different Entities, Different Rules__

ItΓÇÖs vital to distinguish between acute and chronic pain, as they operate under fundamentally different rules and serve different purposes\.

- __Acute pain__ is the bodyΓÇÖs normal, time\-limited response to injury or illness\. ItΓÇÖs a crucial alarm system\. When you sprain your ankle, the pain tells you to rest it, protect it, and allow it to heal\. ItΓÇÖs typically well defined and localized, and it resolves as the underlying cause heals, usually within days, weeks, or at most, a few months\. Treatments are often aimed at addressing the injury and providing temporary relief\.
- __Chronic pain__, by contrast, is pain that persists beyond the normal healing time, typically defined as lasting or recurring for more than three to six months\. Unlike acute pain, it often serves no ongoing protective purpose\. Instead, the pain system itself can become the problem\. The ΓÇ£alarmΓÇ¥ gets stuck in the ΓÇ£onΓÇ¥ position, or even becomes more sensitive over time, a phenomenon known as sensitization, which weΓÇÖll delve into in the next chapter\. Chronic pain is different in character as well\. It can be more diffuse than acute pain, is often harder to pinpoint, and is frequently accompanied by other symptoms like fatigue, sleep disturbances, and mood changes\.

One of the biggest shifts in pain science since 2011 is a deeper understanding of how acute pain can transition into chronic pain\. ItΓÇÖs not simply a matter of duration\. Factors like the severity of the initial injury, genetic predisposition, psychological distress at the time of injury, and even social factors can influence this transition\. This knowledge is paving the way for earlier, more targeted interventions aimed at preventing pain from becoming chronic in the first place\.

__The ΓÇ£Pain RevolutionΓÇ¥: Understanding Is Power__

The term ΓÇ£pain revolutionΓÇ¥ has been popularized by researchers and clinicians, particularly in Australia, to describe the paradigm shift in how we educate both patients and healthcare professionals about pain\. A key tenet of this revolution is that understanding pain can in itself be a powerful __analgesic__\. Chronic pain doesnΓÇÖt necessarily mean ongoing damage\. Instead, your nervous system has become overprotective and sensitized\. Understanding that there is no ongoing damage can reduce fear, anxiety, and catastrophizing\. This knowledge empowers people to engage more confidently in active rehabilitation strategies like exercise, knowing that hurt does not always equal harm\.

This new understanding moves away from a purely structural or biomedical viewΓÇöthe idea that pain is always a direct reflection of tissue damage visible on an X\-ray or MRI\. While structural issues can certainly contribute, especially in conditions like __arthritis__, many people with chronic pain have scans that donΓÇÖt fully explain their pain levels\. Conversely, many people have ΓÇ£abnormalitiesΓÇ¥ on scans \(like disc bulges\) but experience no pain at all\. This highlights that pain is constructed in the brain based on a wide array of inputs, including sensory information, thoughts, emotions, memories, and context\.

As a physician, explaining these concepts to my patients is now a fundamental part of my practice\. When someone with chronic low back pain whose magnetic resonance imaging \(MRI\) shows only ΓÇ£age\-appropriate changesΓÇ¥ understands that their ongoing pain is likely due to a sensitized nervous system rather than a dangerously damaged spine, they are initially confused\. Patients ask, ΓÇ£Why do I have so much pain, then, if my scan is relatively normal?ΓÇ¥ Fully grasping the concept of having a sensitized nervous system, though, can be liberating\. It shifts the focus from a hopeless search for a ΓÇ£fixΓÇ¥ for their spine to an active engagement in strategies that can calm the nervous system and restore function\.

In the chapters that follow, we will build on this foundational understanding, exploring the specific mechanisms that drive chronic pain, the wide\-ranging impact it can have on your life, and, most importantly, the diverse and evidence\-based strategies you can use to manage it effectively and reclaim your life\. The journey of living with chronic pain is unique to each person, but the principles of understanding your pain, engaging in active self\-management, and cultivating resilience are universal\. Welcome to the forefront of modern pain understanding: a place where knowledge truly is power\.

__Chapter 2: The Science of Hurt: How Your Body and Brain Create and Perpetuate Pain__ 

In the previous chapter, we established that chronic pain is far more than a simple signal of tissue damage\. ItΓÇÖs a complex experience shaped by biological, psychological, and social factors\. Now, letΓÇÖs delve deeper into the ΓÇ£bioΓÇ¥ part of the equation, exploring the intricate ways your body and nervous system detect, transmit, and process information that can lead to the experience of pain\. Understanding these mechanisms is crucial for grasping why chronic pain can be so persistent and why modern treatments are designed the way they are\.

When I first started experiencing severe pain from my AS during medical school, my initial understanding, even as a budding physician, was fairly basic: inflammation in my spine was causing pain\. While true, this was only part of the story\. The journey of a ΓÇ£dangerΓÇ¥ signal from the tissues to the conscious experience of pain is a fascinating, multistage process\. At each stage things can change, particularly in the transition to chronic pain\.

__Nociception: The BodyΓÇÖs Danger Detection System__

Our bodies are equipped with a sophisticated network of specialized sensory nerve endings called __nociceptors__\. Think of them as tiny danger detectors, constantly monitoring our tissues for potentially harmful stimuliΓÇöintense pressure, extreme temperatures \(hot or cold\), or chemicals released by damaged cells or inflammation\. Unlike other sensory receptors that tell us about light touch or normal warmth, nociceptors have a high threshold; they fire \(activate\) only when a stimulus is intense enough to threaten or cause tissue damage\.

![A diagram of a human body](data:image/png;base64,)

Created in  [https://BioRender\.com](https://biorender.com)

Nociceptors are the nerve endings of two main types of nerve fibers that carry danger signals toward the spinal cord and brain\.:

- __A\-delta fibers:__ These are thinly myelinated \(myelin is an insulating sheath that speeds up nerve conduction\) and transmit signals quickly\. They are responsible for the initial, sharp, well\-localized pain that, for example, makes you instantly pull your hand away from a hot surface\.
- __C fibers:__ These are unmyelinated and therefore transmit signals more slowly\. They are responsible for the dull, aching, burning, and more diffuse pain that often follows the initial sharp pain\. This is the pain that lingers for minutes to hours after a burn\.

These nociceptors are part of the peripheral nervous system, a critical part of the nervous system that includes all of the nerves and ganglia \(clusters of nerve cells\) that lie outside of the central nervous system, which is made up of the brain and spinal cord\. Think of the peripheral nervous system as the "cabling" that connects the brain and spinal cord to the rest of the body\.

When nociceptors are activated, they donΓÇÖt automatically ΓÇ£transmit pain\.ΓÇ¥ Rather, they transmit information about the presence and intensity of a potentially threatening injury\. This information travels as electrical signals along the A\-delta and C fibers to the __dorsal horn__ of the spinal cord\. The dorsal horn is a critical processing station that functions like a complex switchboard where incoming signals are modulated, or altered, before being relayed up to the brain \(see figure below\)\. As you will discover over the next few pages, the dorsal horn is a critical area where chronic pain can begin\.

![A diagram of the nervous system](data:image/png;base64,)

Created in  [https://BioRender\.com](https://biorender.com)

__Peripheral Sensitization: Turning Up the Volume at the Source__

Imagine you have a sunburn\. Even a light touch or warm water on the sunburned skin can feel intensely painful\. This is an everyday example of __peripheral sensitization__\. In this state, the nociceptors in the affected area become much more responsive\. They lower their activation threshold, meaning they now fire in response to stimuli that wouldnΓÇÖt normally be painful \(like a gentle touchΓÇöthis is called __allodynia__\) or react more intensely to stimuli that are normally painful \(this is called __hyperalgesia__\)\.

Peripheral sensitization is a normal, adaptive response in the short term\. When tissues are injured, an ΓÇ£inflammatory soupΓÇ¥ of chemicals \(like prostaglandins, bradykinin, and __cytokines__\) is released at the site\. These chemicals directly activate nociceptors and make them more sensitive\. This heightened sensitivity encourages you to protect the injured area to promote healing\. For example, the throbbing pain of a sprained ankle due to peripheral sensitization discourages you from putting weight on it\.

However, if the inflammation persists, as it does in conditions like RA or my AS, or if the injury is severe, peripheral sensitization can become a chronic problem, contributing to ongoing pain even after the initial healing\. This is one reason why __anti\-inflammatory__ medications can be helpful for some types of pain because they reduce the production of these sensitizing chemicals\. 

*Research update:* Our understanding of the specific ion channels and receptors on nociceptors that are altered during peripheral sensitization has grown immensely\. \(Receptors are proteins on the surface of cells that, when certain substances bind to them, can trigger channels in the cell to open and activate a series of events\.\) For instance, channels like TRPV1 \(the ΓÇ£capsaicin receptor,ΓÇ¥ activated by heat and chili peppers\) and sodium channels such as NaV1\.7, NaV1\.8, and NaV1\.9 have been identified as important for peripheral sensitization\. This has led to research into new drugs that target these channels, aiming for more precise pain relief with fewer side effects\. For example, NaV1\.7 inhibitors are a significant area of ongoing research, as people with rare genetic mutations causing a nonfunctional NaV1\.7 channel cannot feel pain\.

![A diagram of a cell membrane

AI-generated content may be incorrect.](data:image/png;base64,)

__Created in  __[__https://BioRender\.com__](https://biorender.com)

__Central Sensitization: The Nervous System on High Alert__

While peripheral sensitization occurs at the site of injury or inflammation, central sensitization is a more profound and often more problematic change that occurs within the central nervous system\. This sensitization takes place primarily in the dorsal horn of the spinal cord and also in the brain\. If peripheral nociceptors continue to bombard the spinal cord with danger signals over an extended period, the neurons in the dorsal horn can become hyperexcitable\. They effectively learn to be more sensitive\. 

I use the analogy of studying for tests in school with my patients\. If you study all the time, you get better at memorizing information\. Unfortunately, if you hurt all the time, the dorsal horn gets better at processing and communicating pain\.

Think of it like this: The ΓÇ£volume knobΓÇ¥ for pain processing in the spinal cord and brain gets turned up and stuck at a higher level\. As a result:

- __Pain becomes more widespread:__ Pain may be felt in areas distant from the original site of injury or even on the opposite side of the body\. This is because the receptive fields of the spinal cord neurons enlarge and spread\. This means the sensitized area of the dorsal horn gets larger, so the brain gets signals that more of the body hurts\.
- __Pain thresholds decrease:__ Stimuli that were previously harmless, like light touch or normal movement, can now be perceived as painful \(allodynia\)\. When I am concerned about a patient having allodynia, I often ask if they find showers to be painful\. With allodynia, even the water hitting the skin in what would otherwise be a relaxing shower can be uncomfortable\.
- __Pain intensity increases:__ Painful stimuli are perceived as being much more intense than they normally would be \(hyperalgesia\)\. 

There was a landmark study some years ago that used thumb screws as a standard way of applying pain\. The researchers compared normal controlsΓÇöpatients who did not have __fibromyalgia__ΓÇöwith patients who had fibromyalgia\. As expected, patients with fibromyalgia experienced severe pain with much less pressure on their thumbs \(hyperalgesia\) than the pressure required for normal controls to experience severe pain\. 

What was most important about this study, though, was that the researchers also used a specialized MRI scan called functional MRI \(fMRI\) to assess brain activity during the application of the thumb screws\. Brain activity for normal controls experiencing high pressure on their thumbs was almost exactly the same as that for patients with fibromyalgia experiencing only mild pressure\. The researchers proved that the pain experienced by those with fibromyalgia was just as severe as that by normal controls despite much less pressure on their thumbs\.

In short, this study proved that in fibromyalgia patients with hyperalgesia, their pain is real\.

- __Pain persists:__ Pain can continue long after the initial injury has healed or the inflammatory process has subsided\.

Several key mechanisms contribute to central sensitization:

1. __ΓÇ£Wind\-upΓÇ¥ and long\-term potentiation \(LTP\):__ Repeated activation of C fibers progressively increases the electrical response of dorsal horn neurons, something called ΓÇ£wind\-up\.ΓÇ¥ If this barrage of pain signaling continues, it can trigger more lasting changes in the strength of connections between neurons, similar to the processes involved in learning and memory in the brain\. Essentially, the pain pathways become more efficient at transmitting signals\. Chronic pain teaches the nervous system to be good at pain signaling\.
2. __Activation of NMDA receptors:__ A key receptor involved in wind\-up and LTP is the *N*\-methyl\-d\-aspartate \(NMDA\) receptor\. Normally, this receptor is blocked by a magnesium ion\. However, with intense or prolonged painful input, this block can be removed, allowing the receptor to become highly active, leading to increased excitability of the neuron\. This is why some older medications like ketamine, an NMDA receptor antagonist, are being explored for certain chronic pain statesΓÇöthey bind to the receptor where the magnesium ion was, decreasing the excitability of the neuron and thereby decreasing the pain signals to the brain\.
3. __Loss of inhibition:__ The spinal cord has its own pain\-inhibiting pathways\. In central sensitization, these inhibitory processes can become less effective, further tipping the balance toward increased pain transmission\.
4. __Glial cell activation:__ This significant area of research has exploded since 2011\. Glial cells \(such as microglia and astrocytes\) in the spinal cord and brain were once thought to be mere support cells for neurons\. We now know they are active players in pain processing\. When activated by signals from hyperactive neurons or injury, these glial cells release a host of proinflammatory substances \(cytokines, chemokines\) that can further sensitize neurons, amplify pain signals, and contribute to the maintenance of chronic pain\. They are now considered major targets for new pain therapies\.

Central sensitization is not ΓÇ£all in your headΓÇ¥ in a psychological sense but instead is a real, neurophysiological change in how your central nervous system processes information\. It is thought to be a primary driver of pain in conditions like fibromyalgia, irritable bowel syndrome \(IBS\), temporomandibular disorders \(TMD\), some types of chronic low back pain, chronic headaches, and __complex regional pain syndrome __\(__CRPS__\)__\.__

__Nociplastic Pain: A New Term for a Familiar Concept__

Reflecting the growing understanding of central sensitization and other altered pain\-processing mechanisms, the IASP introduced the term __nociplastic pain__ in 2017\. ItΓÇÖs defined as ΓÇ£pain that arises from altered nociception despite no clear evidence of actual or threatened tissue damage causing the activation of peripheral nociceptors or evidence for disease or lesion of the somatosensory system causing the pain\.ΓÇ¥

Essentially, nociplastic pain occurs when the primary problem lies in how sensory information is *processed* within the central nervous system, rather than in ongoing damage in the periphery nervous system \(__nociceptive pain__\) or damage to the nerves themselves \(__neuropathic pain__\)\. Fibromyalgia is often cited as a classic example of nociplastic pain\. Patients with nociplastic pain typically exhibit widespread pain, tenderness, fatigue, sleep disturbances, and cognitive symptoms, all hallmarks of a sensitized central nervous system\. Recognizing nociplastic pain as a distinct category helps clinicians choose more appropriate treatments, often focusing on strategies that aim to ΓÇ£retrainΓÇ¥ or calm the nervous system, such as graded exercise \(exercise that gradually increases in intensity\), certain medications that modulate __neurotransmitters__ \(chemical messengers that transmit signals throughout the brain and body\), and mind\-body therapies\.

__The BrainΓÇÖs Role: Perception, Modulation, and Neuroplasticity__

The signals relayed from the dorsal horn travel up the spinal cord via various tracts \(like the spinothalamic tract\) to multiple areas in the brain\. There isnΓÇÖt one single ΓÇ£pain centerΓÇ¥ in the brain\. Instead, a distributed network of brain regions, often called the ΓÇ£pain matrixΓÇ¥ or ΓÇ£pain neuromatrix,ΓÇ¥ is involved\. These regions include the following:

- __Thalamus:__ A relay station for sensory information\.
- __Somatosensory cortex:__ Involved in processing pain and identifying its sensory qualities \(e\.g\., sharp, burning\)\.
- __Insula and anterior cingulate cortex \(ACC\):__ Involved in the emotional aspects of painΓÇöthe unpleasantness, suffering, and motivation to escape the pain\.
- __Prefrontal cortex:__ Involved in cognitive evaluation, attention, meaning, and decision\-making related to pain\.
- __Limbic system \(e\.g\., amygdala, hippocampus\):__ Involved in emotion, fear, and memory related to pain\.

![A diagram of a brain](data:image/png;base64,)

Created in  [https://BioRender\.co](https://biorender.co)m

Crucially, the brain is not a passive receiver of signals\. It actively interprets and modulates all the signals it receives\. Your thoughts, beliefs, emotions, attention, and past experiences can all profoundly influence how intensely you perceive pain\. For example, if you are highly anxious or focused on your pain, it may feel worse\. If you are distracted or engaged in a pleasant activity, it may feel less intense\.

The brain also has powerful __descending pain modulation __pathways\. These pathways originate in different areas of the brain and extend down to the dorsal horn of the spinal cord\. They can release neurotransmitters like serotonin \(our bodyΓÇÖs natural mood booster\), norepinephrine \(our bodyΓÇÖs fight\-or\-flight regulator\), and __endogenous opioids__ \(our bodyΓÇÖs natural painkillers, like endorphins\) that can inhibit or help transmit pain signals at the spinal level\. In chronic pain, these descending inhibitory pathways can become dysfunctional, contributing to the persistence of pain\. Many treatments, including certain medications \(like serotoninΓÇônorepinephrine reuptake inhibitors, or __SNRIs__, that boost serotonin and norepinephrine\) and even exercise, are thought to work in part by enhancing these descending inhibitory controls\.

*Research update:* __Neuroplasticity__, the brainΓÇÖs ability to adapt and change based upon experiences and stimuli, can be both harmful and beneficial for those living with chronic pain\. While maladaptive plasticity can facilitate  chronic pain \(e\.g\., strengthening of pain pathways in the dorsal horn\), positive neuroplasticity can offer a chance of improvement\. Therapeutic approaches like PNE, cognitive therapies, mindfulness, and graded motor imagery aim to harness the brainΓÇÖs capacity for change to ΓÇ£retrainΓÇ¥ pain processing and reduce pain perception\. Advanced neuroimaging techniques like fMRI allow researchers to visualize these changes in brain activity and structure in response to pain and treatment, providing deeper insights into these mechanisms\.

Understanding the science of how your nervous system processes danger signals and how these processes can go awry in chronic pain is the first step toward regaining a sense of control\. Knowledge helps to demystify the experience, replacing fear and confusion with understanding\. This knowledge forms the foundation for the strategies we will discuss in the rest of this bookΓÇöstrategies designed to target these very mechanisms, to soothe the sensitized nervous system, and to help you live a fuller life despite pain\.

__Chapter 3: When Good Alarms Go Bad: Why Untreated or Undertreated Pain Matters__ 

In the first edition of this book, I emphasized the importance of taking your pain medicine, not just for immediate relief, but to prevent things from getting worse\. I spoke about the problematic cycle of muscle spasm and the potential for pain processing centers in the spinal cord to become overactive\. A decade later, armed with a much deeper understanding of pain neurobiology, this advice is more critical than ever\. Untreated or inadequately treated pain isnΓÇÖt just an unpleasant experience to be endured\. Persistent pain can trigger a cascade of physiological changes that fundamentally alter your nervous system, effectively making it better at creating and sustaining pain\. When the bodyΓÇÖs normally protective alarm system goes haywire, the consequences can be profound and far\-reaching\.

LetΓÇÖs revisit and expand on why allowing pain to go unchecked can lead to a host of problems, turning what might have been a temporary issue into a persistent, life\-altering condition\.

__The Vicious Cycle of Muscle Spasm and Guarding: The Splinting Reflex Revisited__

As I mentioned in 2011, the __splinting reflex__ is a basic spinal cord response to pain\. If you injure a limb, nearby muscles instinctively tighten to immobilize and protect the area\. In the short term, for an acute injury like a broken bone, this is helpful\. However, when pain becomes chronic, this protective guarding and spasm can persist long after itΓÇÖs useful, becoming a significant source of pain itself\.

Think about chronic low back pain\. The initial pain might stem from a disc issue or facet joint arthritis\. In response, the surrounding paraspinal muscles tense up\. If this tension becomes chronic, these muscles can become fatigued and develop __trigger points__ \(hyperirritable knots\), and their blood flow can be compromised\. These constantly contracted muscles then start sending their own nociceptive \(danger\) signals to the spinal cord\. This new source of input further sensitizes the dorsal horn neurons, which, in turn, can command even more muscle guarding\. It becomes a self\-perpetuating vicious cycle: Pain leads to muscle spasm, which leads to more pain, which leads to more spasm\.

*Research update:* Modern research has further defined this process\. We now understand that chronic muscle guarding can lead to changes in muscle fiber type, reduced muscle endurance, and altered movement patterns\. These altered biomechanics can then place abnormal stress on other joints and tissues, potentially creating new sources of pain\. Furthermore, the constant barrage of nociceptive input from chronically tensed muscles is a powerful driver for the central sensitization processes we discussed in chapter 2\. Breaking this cycle often requires a multipronged approach, including treatments that directly address muscle tension \(like physical therapy, massage, or certain medications\) alongside strategies to reduce the underlying pain and calm the nervous system\.

Many of my patients struggle with chronic muscle spasm and the resulting pain\. Recognizing the spasm itself as a driver of their pain allows them to work on strategies to limit muscle spasm, such as taking a muscle relaxer every night, lying down for five to ten minutes \(not necessarily sleeping\) a few times a day to unload the spine, and adding a daily stretching regimen to their routine\.

__Dorsal Horn Rewiring: When Wind\-Up Becomes a Permanent Fixture__

In chapter 2, we touched on wind\-up and long\-term potentiation \(LTP\) in the dorsal horn neurons of the spinal cord as critical mechanisms in developing central sensitization\. Untreated or undertreated persistent pain is like constantly fanning the flames of these processes\.

Imagine the dorsal horn neurons as gatekeepers\. With ongoing nociceptive input, these gatekeepers donΓÇÖt just get temporarily overwhelmed\. They can undergo structural and functional changes that make them more efficient at transmitting danger signals and less responsive to the bodyΓÇÖs natural pain\-inhibiting mechanisms\. These changes include:

- __Increased excitatory neurotransmitters:__ The synapses \(connections\) between incoming nerve fibers and dorsal horn neurons can start releasing more excitatory neurotransmitters like glutamate and __substance P__\.
- __More receptors:__ The dorsal horn neurons themselves can sprout more receptors for these excitatory neurotransmitters, making them even more responsive to incoming signals\.
- __Altered gene expression:__ Persistent pain can actually change which genes are turned on or off within these neurons, leading to long\-lasting alterations in their structure, function, and chemistry\. For example, genes responsible for producing pro\-inflammatory molecules or excitatory receptors might be upregulated \(meaning cells make more inflammatory molecules and receptors\)\.
- __Reduced inhibition:__ The local inhibitory interneurons in the dorsal horn, which normally help to dampen down excessive signaling \(using neurotransmitters like GABA and glycine\), can become less effective or even die off in the face of chronic pain\. This loss of ΓÇ£braking powerΓÇ¥ further contributes to hyperexcitability\.

The original caveat I mentioned in 2011 Some pain experts think narcotic pain medication might itself lead to increased dorsal horn activity \(a phenomenon known as __opioid\-induced hyperalgesia__, or OIH\), particularly with long\-term, high\-dose opioid use\. While __opioids__ can be very effective for acute pain and some types of chronic pain, their prolonged use can, paradoxically, sensitize pain pathways in some people, making them more sensitive to pain\. This doesnΓÇÖt negate the importance of appropriate pain management, but it highlights the need for careful, individualized prescribing and a multimodal approach \(combination of therapies\) that doesnΓÇÖt rely solely on opioids\.

__The ΓÇ£Pain BrainΓÇ¥: Structural and Functional Changes in Your Control Center__

The consequences of unchecked chronic pain extend far beyond the spinal cord\. The brain, the ultimate interpreter and creator of the pain experience, also undergoes significant changes during chronic pain\. This phenomenon is sometimes referred to as the ΓÇ£pain brainΓÇ¥ or, more accurately, alterations in the pain neuromatrix\.

*Research update:* Advanced neuroimaging techniques like voxel\-based morphometry \(VBM\) and diffusion tensor imaging \(DTI\) have allowed scientists to observe structural changes in the brains of people with chronic pain\. Studies have shown:

- __Gray matter reduction:__ Reduced gray matter volume \(which contains neuron cell bodies\) has been observed in areas like the prefrontal cortex \(involved in cognition, decision\-making, and emotion regulation\), thalamus \(a sensory relay station\), anterior cingulate cortex \(emotional aspects of pain\), and insula \(__interoception__ΓÇöthe sense of the internal state of the bodyΓÇöand pain perception\)\. These reductions are not necessarily permanent cell death; they can reflect changes in neuron size, synaptic density, or blood flow\. Encouragingly, some research suggests these changes can be reversible with effective pain treatment\.
- __White matter alterations:__ Changes have also been found in white matter tracts \(the ΓÇ£wiringΓÇ¥ that connects different brain regions\), potentially affecting how efficiently these areas communicate\.
- __Functional reorganization:__ Beyond structural changes, the way brain regions activate and communicate with each other can also be altered\. For instance, there might be increased activity in brain areas associated with emotion and fear \(like the amygdala\)\. The brainΓÇÖs default mode network \(active when we are at rest and relaxing\) can also show altered connectivity in chronic pain, potentially contributing to difficulties with attention and increasing self\-focus on pain\.

These brain changes can help explain many of the associated symptoms of chronic pain, such as cognitive difficulties \(ΓÇ£brain fogΓÇ¥ or ΓÇ£pain fog,ΓÇ¥ which weΓÇÖll discuss in part 2\), mood disturbances, and sleep problems\. 

The pain brain becomes highly attuned to pain, constantly scanning for threats, which can be exhausting and make it harder to engage with other aspects of life\.

__Reinforcing Sensitization and Creating ΓÇ£Pain MemoriesΓÇ¥__

Just as your brain learns and remembers facts and skills, it can also learn pain, especially if the pain is intense, prolonged, and associated with strong negative emotions\. Every time you experience pain, especially if itΓÇÖs severe and uncontrolled, it reinforces the neural pathways involved in producing that pain\. This is where the concept of ΓÇ£pain memoriesΓÇ¥ comes in\.

While not a literal recording of a past painful event like an autobiographical memory, this concept refers to the lasting neuroplastic changes in pain pathways that make them easier to activate in the future\. The nervous system becomes more efficient at producing pain, sometimes even in response to minimal or no ongoing peripheral input\. This is why, as I mentioned in the original book, some patients with fibromyalgia experience pain signals being sent from the dorsal horn to the brain even without an obvious injury\. Their system has become sensitized and has learned to amplify signals\.

Letting pain run rampant continuously trains your nervous system to become better at being in pain\. Early and effective pain management aims to interrupt this learning process, preventing these maladaptive pain pathways\.

__Systemic Consequences: Beyond the Nervous System__

*Research update:*

The impact of untreated chronic pain doesnΓÇÖt stop at the nervous system\. It can have ripple effects throughout the body, including:

- __Immune dysregulation and chronic inflammation:__ Chronic pain is often associated with a state of low\-grade systemic inflammation\. Stress hormones released due to persistent pain can dysregulate the immune system\. This can create a vicious cycle, as inflammation itself can sensitize nociceptors and contribute to central sensitization\.
- __Metabolic changes:__ Chronic pain can influence metabolism, potentially contributing to weight gain \(due to reduced activity and comfort eating\) or weight loss \(due to nausea or loss of appetite\)\. ThereΓÇÖs also emerging research on links between chronic pain and conditions like insulin resistance\.
- __Cardiovascular strain:__ Chronic pain acts as a significant physiological stressor, which can elevate heart rate and blood pressure over time, potentially increasing the risk of cardiovascular problems\.
- __Endocrine system disruption:__ The hypothalamic\-pituitary\-adrenal \(HPA\) axis, our central stress response system, can become dysregulated by chronic pain\. This dysregulation can lead to abnormal cortisol levels, which can affect energy, mood, and immune function\.

__The Importance of Maintaining Function and Avoiding Withdrawal__

This brings me back to a critical point from my original advice: the danger of withdrawing from life in an attempt to limit pain\. As pain becomes more entrenched and the nervous system more sensitized, itΓÇÖs natural to want to do less, to avoid activities that might provoke pain\. However, this often leads to a downward spiral\. Reduced activity leads to deconditioning, muscle weakness, joint stiffness, and weight gain, all of which can further exacerbate pain\. Social withdrawal can lead to isolation, depression, and anxiety, which are potent amplifiers of pain\.

I often ask my patients who are struggling with chronic pain if they have been doing anything outside their houses\. For those with the most severe pain, I ask if they have even been getting out of bed\. The danger of limiting activities is real\. When patients stop engaging with life, they not only miss out on important experiences but also inadvertently worsen their physiological state\. Physical therapy appointments are missed, social connections dwindle, and the world shrinks\. This inactivity and isolation provide fertile ground for pain to become even more dominant\.

Managing pain effectively is not just about feeling better in the moment\. Effective management is more about maintaining your ability to function, to engage with your life, to stay active within your limits, and to prevent the cascade of negative physiological changes that can occur when good alarms go bad\. ItΓÇÖs about protecting your nervous system, your brain, and your overall health from the long\-term consequences of unchecked pain\. This doesnΓÇÖt mean pushing through severe pain heedlessly but instead working with your healthcare team to find a sustainable plan that reduces pain enough to allow you to reengage with activities that bring meaning and joy to your life, thereby actively countering the detrimental effects of a sensitized system\.

In the following chapters, we will explore the multifaceted ways chronic pain can impact your daily life and then delve into the wide range of modern strategiesΓÇömedical, psychological, and lifestyle\-basedΓÇöthat can help you manage your pain and break these vicious cycles\.

__Chapter 4: The Many Faces of Pain: Types, Syndromes, and Their Unique Challenges__ 

As weΓÇÖve established, pain is not monolithic\. Just as there are many shades of color, there are many ΓÇ£flavorsΓÇ¥ of pain, each with its own underlying mechanisms, characteristic sensations, and, importantly, implications for treatment\. In my years as a rheumatologist, one of the first crucial steps in helping a patient is to understand not just *that* they hurt, but *how* they hurt and *why*\. Accurately identifying the type of pain is paramount because it guides us toward the most effective and targeted management strategies\. A treatment that works wonders for one type of pain might be ineffective or even detrimental for another\.

Since the early 2000s, the classification of pain has become more refined\. Broadly, chronic pain is now commonly categorized into three main types based on the underlying neurobiology: nociceptive pain, neuropathic pain, and the more recently recognized nociplastic pain\. People may experience a combination of these, leading to whatΓÇÖs known as __mixed pain states__\.

LetΓÇÖs explore these categories and some common pain syndromes that illustrate their unique challenges\.

__1\. Nociceptive Pain: The ΓÇ£TraditionalΓÇ¥ Pain of Tissue Damage__

Nociceptive pain is perhaps the most straightforward type of pain to understand\. It arises when nociceptors \(those specialized sensory receptors we discussed in chapter 2\) are activated in response to actual or threatened damage to nonneural tissues \(e\.g\., skin, muscles, bones, joints, internal organs\)\. This is the pain you feel from a cut, a bruise, a broken bone, or inflammation in a joint\.

- __Mechanism:__ Direct stimulation of intact nociceptors \(pain nerves\) by harmful thermal, mechanical, or chemical stimuli\.
- __Common examples:__ 
	- __Osteoarthritis \(OA\):__ Pain from the breakdown of cartilage and underlying bone in joints\.
	- __Rheumatoid arthritis \(RA\):__ Pain due to autoimmune inflammation attacking the joints\.
	- __Mechanical low back pain:__ Pain from strain on muscles, ligaments, or discs\.
	- __Postoperative pain:__ Pain resulting from surgical trauma to tissues \(including incisions\)\.
	- __Burns:__ Pain from thermal injury to the skin\.
- __Typical descriptors:__ Often described as aching, throbbing, cramping, sharp with movement, dull, or tender, nociceptive pain is usually well localized, though it can become more diffuse if widespread inflammation is present\.
- *Research updates: *
	- For OA, research has highlighted both the role of low\-grade inflammation even in what was once considered purely ΓÇ£wear\-and\-tearΓÇ¥ disease and the complex interplay between structural changes seen on imaging and the pain experienced\. The central nervous system can also become sensitized in chronic OA\.
	- In inflammatory arthritis like RA and AS, our understanding of specific inflammatory cytokines \(like TNF\-alpha, interleukin\-6 \[IL\-6\], IL\-17, and others\) has led to the development of highly effective biologic therapies that target these molecules, significantly reducing pain and preventing joint damage for many\.

__2\. Neuropathic Pain: Pain from Nerve Injury or Disease__

Neuropathic pain is distinctly different from nociceptive pain\. It results from damage or disease affecting the nervous system itself, in particular the nerves, spinal cord, and/or brain regions that normally transmit and process sensory information\. The pain is not coming from stimulated nociceptors in the periphery but rather from abnormal functioning of the damaged nerve pathways\.

- __Mechanism:__ Injury or dysfunction within the peripheral or central nervous system leading to spontaneous nerve firing, altered signal processing, or loss of normal inhibitory mechanisms\.
- __Common examples:__ 
	- __Diabetic neuropathy:__ Nerve damage caused by high blood sugar, often affecting the feet and legs\.
	- __Postherpetic neuralgia:__ Persistent pain after an outbreak of shingles\.
	- __Sciatica / radicular pain:__ Pain radiating down the leg due to compression or inflammation of a spinal nerve root \(e\.g\., from a herniated disc\)\.
	- __Trigeminal neuralgia:__ Severe facial pain due to dysfunction of the trigeminal nerve\.
	- __Central poststroke pain:__ Pain arising from damage to pain pathways in the brain after a stroke\.
	- __Complex regional pain syndrome \(CRPS\) type II:__ Occurs after identifiable nerve injury\.
- __Typical descriptors:__ Often described as unusual and unpleasant sensations such as burning, shooting, stabbing, electric shocks, tingling, pins and needles, numbness, or itching\. The pain can be spontaneous or evoked by light touch \(allodynia\) or even mild temperature changes\.
- *Research updates: *
	- ThereΓÇÖs a greater understanding of the role of specific ion channels \(e\.g\., sodium, calcium channels\) on damaged nerves that contribute to their hyperexcitability\. This is driving research into more selective channel blockers\.
	- We now know that a key driver of neuropathic pain occurs when neuroinflammation involving immune cells and glial cells interact with neurons within the damaged nerve or spinal cord\.
	- Researchers are identifying genetic factors that influence susceptibility to neuropathic pain and response to treatment\.

__3\. Nociplastic Pain: Pain from Altered Nociception Without Clear Tissue or Nerve Damage__

As mentioned in chapter 2, nociplastic pain is the newest major category of pain, formally introduced by the IASP in 2017, though the underlying concepts of central sensitization have been developing for much longer\. Nociplastic pain arises from altered nociception \(the processing of danger signals\) despite no tissue damage and no damage to the nervous  system causing the pain\. In simpler terms, the pain system itself has become dysfunctional and oversensitive, primarily due to changes in the central nervous system \(central sensitization\)\.

- __Mechanism:__ Primarily driven by central nervous system sensitization, which involves changes in neurotransmitter levels, receptor function, glial cell activity, and altered descending pain modulation; these changes amplify pain signals and reduce the ability to inhibit them\.
- __Common examples:__ 
	- __Fibromyalgia:__ Characterized by widespread musculoskeletal pain and tenderness, poor sleep, fatigue,, cognitive dysfunction \(ΓÇ£fibro fogΓÇ¥\), and tender points\. This is often considered the archetypal nociplastic pain condition\.
	- __Irritable bowel syndrome \(IBS\) and painful bladder syndrome:__ __Visceral pain __\(pain deep inside the body\)\. where gut/bladder hypersensitivity and altered central processing play a major role\.
	- __Temporomandibular disorder \(TMD\):__ Some forms of jaw pain\.
	- __Nonspecific chronic low back pain / neck pain:__ A significant nociplastic component is often involved where pain persists despite no clear structural or neurological cause fully explaining the symptoms\.
	- __Chronic widespread pain:__ A broader term for pain experienced in multiple body regions\.
	- __Some chronic headaches:__ Tension\-type headaches and chronic migraine can have significant nociplastic components, especially when they become very frequent\.
	- __CRPS type I:__ Where there is no identifiable nerve injury but symptoms of severe pain, allodynia, swelling, and autonomic changes suggest altered central processing\.
- __Typical descriptors:__ Often described as widespread, diffuse, aching, and deep\. ItΓÇÖs frequently accompanied by heightened sensitivity to touch, pressure, sound, light, or even smells\. Fatigue, poor sleep, and cognitive issues are very common __comorbidities__\.
- *Research updates: *
	- Recognizing and defining nociplastic pain itself is a major advancement that allows for more targeted research and treatment development\.
	- Neuroimaging studies \(fMRI, positron emission tomography, or PET\) continue to reveal altered brain activity and connectivity in conditions like fibromyalgia, confirming central nervous system changes\.
	- Research into biomarkers \(e\.g\., inflammatory chemicals in cerebrospinal fluid, specific DNA testing results , MRI and CT results that show evidence of chronic pain\), though definitive biomarkers are still elusive\.
	- Researchers are investigating the role of glial cell activation and neuroinflammation within the central nervous system in maintaining nociplastic pain\.
	- Treatments often focus on __neuromodulation__ \(e\.g\., certain medications like SNRIs, __gabapentinoids__, and tricyclics\), exercise therapies designed to desensitize the system \(like graded exercise\), and mind\-body approaches \(pain neuroscience education \[PNE\], __cognitive behavioral therapy__ \[CBT\], acceptance and commitment therapy \[ACT\], and __mindfulness\-based stress reduction __\[MBSR\]\)\.

__Mixed Pain States__

The categories of pain we just covered are not always mutually exclusive\. Many people with chronic pain experience mixed pain states, where elements of two or even all three types coexist\. For example:

- Someone with severe osteoarthritis \(nociceptive\) in their knee might compensate by walking differently, leading to chronic muscle strain \(also nociceptive\)\. Over time, the persistent pain signals can lead to central sensitization \(nociplastic features\)\. If they undergo knee replacement surgery, they might develop neuropathic pain if a nerve is inadvertently damaged during the procedure\.
- Chronic low back pain often involves nociceptive components \(from discs, joints, or muscles\), neuropathic components if thereΓÇÖs nerve root compression \(radiculopathy\), and significant nociplastic components due to central sensitization from the long\-standing pain\.

__A Closer Look at Common Pain Syndromes__

- __Ankylosing spondylitis \(AS\):__ As someone living with AS, I know its pain intimately\. It is primarily an *inflammatory nociceptive* pain, driven by the immune system attacking the spine and sacroiliac joints\. Biologic medications that target specific inflammatory pathways have revolutionized treatment and can dramatically reduce this pain\. However, if AS is not well controlled, the persistent inflammation and pain can lead to secondary muscle spasm \(more nociceptive pain\) and, over time, can certainly contribute to central sensitization, adding a *nociplastic* overlay\. Studies suggest that almost half of those living with AS develop *nociplastic* pain over time\. Early and aggressive treatment of the underlying inflammation is key\.
- __Osteoarthritis \(OA\):__ Traditionally viewed as a ΓÇ£wear\-and\-tearΓÇ¥ *nociceptive* condition, OA pain arises from cartilage degradation, bone changes, and inflammation within the joint\. However, we now recognize that many people with chronic OA pain also develop *nociplastic* features, particularly central sensitization\. This helps explain why the degree of pain doesnΓÇÖt always correlate well with X\-ray findings\. Treatments range from lifestyle modifications \(weight loss, exercise\) and simple analgesics to joint injections and, ultimately, joint replacement\. Therapies addressing central sensitization are also increasingly used\.
- __Rheumatoid arthritis \(RA\):__ Like AS, RA is an autoimmune disease causing *inflammatory nociceptive* pain in multiple joints\. Early diagnosis and treatment with disease\-modifying antirheumatic drugs \(DMARDs\) and biologics are crucial to control inflammation, reduce pain, and prevent joint destruction\. *Nociplastic* components can also develop if pain persists\.
- __Fibromyalgia:__ This is now widely recognized as a prototypical *nociplastic*__ __pain syndrome\. The primary issue is not ongoing damage in the muscles or joints but rather a dysregulation of pain processing within the central nervous system\. Management focuses on a multimodal approach, including PNE, low\-impact aerobic exercise, stress management, __sleep hygiene__, and medications that modulate neurotransmitters involved in pain processing \(e\.g\., amitriptyline, duloxetine, pregabalin, and others\)\.
- __Chronic migraine and headaches:__ Migraine is a complex neurological disorder\. While acute migraine attacks involve nociceptive mechanisms \(activation of trigeminal nerve pathways supplying nerves to cranial blood vessels and membranes\), the transition to chronic migraine \(fifteen or more headache days per month\) often involves significant *nociplastic* mechanisms and central sensitization\. 

*Research update:* A major breakthrough in migraine treatment has been the development of __CGRP \(calcitonin gene\-related peptide\)__ inhibitors\. CGRP is a neuropeptide \(a chemical messenger produced by neurons\) involved in transmitting pain and widening of blood vessels\. Drugs that block its action have proven highly effective for many people in preventing migraine attacks\. Medication overuse headache, where frequent use of acute pain relievers actually worsens headaches, is another common issue highlighting altered central processing\.

Understanding the type or types of pain you are experiencing is a critical first step in dealing with it\. It allows you and your healthcare team to move beyond simply trying to ΓÇ£kill the painΓÇ¥ to developing a more nuanced and targeted strategy that addresses the underlying mechanisms\. This often involves a combination of therapies tailored to your specific pain profile, its impact on your life, and your personal goals\. This is a theme we will return to throughout this book: Your journey may be complex, but understanding leads to healing\. Knowing the types of pain empowers you to ask the right questions and seek the most appropriate care\.

__Part 2: The Ripple Effect: How Chronic Pain Impacts Your Life__

__Chapter 5: The Physical Burden: Fatigue, Sleep, and Systemic Effects__ 

As anyone who experiences it will attest, living with chronic pain means so much more than just experiencing the sensation of hurting\. The pain itself creates a cascade of physical burdens that can profoundly affect your energy, your ability to rest, your mobility, and even the fundamental workings of your bodyΓÇÖs systems\. In my practice, when a patient tells me about their pain, the conversation almost invariably turns to these associated physical strugglesΓÇöthe pervasive fatigue, the restless nights, the difficulty moving, and a general sense of their body being out of sync or of something being wrong\.

Understanding these physical accompaniments of pain is crucial not only because they add significantly to the overall burden of chronic pain but also because they are often interconnected, creating vicious cycles that can be hard to break\. Addressing these physical impacts is a key component of a comprehensive pain management plan\.

__The Crushing Weight of Chronic Fatigue__

One of the most common and debilitating complaints I hear from patients with chronic pain is fatigueΓÇöan overwhelming sense of exhaustion that isnΓÇÖt relieved by rest or sleep\. This isnΓÇÖt the normal tiredness you feel after a long day of work or strenuous activity\. This is a profound lack of energy that can be present from the moment of waking and can make even simple tasks feel monumental\.

One patient told me recently that she could not muster the energy to attend her grandchildrenΓÇÖs sporting events\. She wanted desperately to go to their soccer and T\-ball games, but the idea of driving there, finding parking, walking to the stands, making conversation with other families, and then having to make her way home was overwhelming\. 

Another patient would go to her grandchildrenΓÇÖs games, but she would spend at least part of the time napping in her car\.

This ΓÇ£pain\-related fatigueΓÇ¥ is not just a psychological consequence of dealing with discomfort\. There is also a significant physiological cause:

- __Persistent inflammation:__ Many chronic pain conditions such as psoriatic arthritis involve ongoing inflammation\. Inflammatory chemicals \(cytokines\) released by the immune system can directly affect the brain and other tissues, leading to feelings of sickness and fatigue \(similar to how you feel when you have the flu\)\.
- __HPA axis dysfunction:__ As we touched on earlier, chronic pain is a significant stressor that can dysregulate the hypothalamic\-pituitary\-adrenal \(HPA\) axisΓÇöour bodyΓÇÖs central stress response system\. This can lead to abnormal levels of cortisol \(the ΓÇ£stress hormoneΓÇ¥\), which over time can contribute to fatigue, sleep problems, and immune dysfunction\.
- __Sleep disruption:__ The intimate and destructive link between pain and poor sleep \(which weΓÇÖll explore next\) is a major contributor to daytime fatigue\. If youΓÇÖre not getting restorative sleep, your body and brain donΓÇÖt have a chance to repair and recharge\.
- __Increased energy expenditure:__ Simply being in pain can be exhausting\. Your body might be constantly tensed \(muscle guarding\), and your brain is working overtime processing pain signals and trying to cope\. This increased physiological and mental effort consumes energy\.
- __Medication side effects:__ Some medications used to manage pain or related conditions can cause drowsiness or fatigue\.
- __Deconditioning:__ Reduced physical activity due to pain leads to muscle weakness and decreased cardiovascular fitness, making any physical exertion feel more tiring\.

*Research update:* Neurobiological research continues to explore the shared pathways and mechanisms linking pain and fatigue\. Studies are investigating how chronic pain alters brain activity in regions associated with motivation, arousal, and energy regulation\. ThereΓÇÖs also growing interest in the role of mitochondrial dysfunction \(mitochondria are the energy powerhouses of our cells\) in pain\-related fatigue\.

__The Unending Dance of Pain and Poor Sleep__

ΓÇ£I canΓÇÖt sleep because of the pain, and then the lack of sleep makes my pain even worse\.ΓÇ¥ This is a sentiment I hear almost daily\. The relationship between pain and sleep is a notorious vicious cycle, and for many, itΓÇÖs one of the most challenging aspects of living with chronic pain\. 

We have all had the experience of feeling both achy and tired after a particularly bad night of sleep\. Those with chronic pain have a bad night of sleep most nights\. Consequently, they are more and more achy over time\.

Pain can disrupt sleep in numerous ways:

- __Difficulty falling asleep:__ Discomfort can make it hard to find a comfortable position or relax enough to drift off\.
- __Frequent awakenings:__ Pain spikes during the night can pull you out of sleep\. Movement during sleep can trigger pain, leading to more awakenings\.
- __Nonrestorative sleep:__ Even if you manage to get some hours of sleep, it may not be deep or restful enough to allow your body and brain to fully recover\. Chronic pain has been shown to alter __sleep architecture__, reducing the amount of deep \(slow\-wave\) sleep and REM \(rapid eye movement\) sleep, each of which are critical for both physical and mental restoration\.

The consequences of this chronic sleep deprivation are significant\. Sleep deprivation doesnΓÇÖt just cause daytime fatigue and fogginess\. It also directly sensitizes the nervous system, lowering your pain threshold and increasing pain intensity\. Lack of sleep can also impair mood, cognitive function, and immune responses, further compounding the overall burden of chronic pain\. Conditions like restless legs syndrome or __sleep apnea__ can also coexist with chronic pain and must be addressed as they can severely exacerbate sleep problems\.

*Research update:* Studies using polysomnography \(sleep studies\) have confirmed significant alterations in the sleep patterns of people with various chronic pain conditions\. ThereΓÇÖs also a greater understanding of how specific neurotransmitters and brain regions involved in both sleep regulation and pain processing overlap, providing clues as to why this connection is so strong\.

__Reduced Mobility and the Downward Spiral of Deconditioning__

When movement hurts, our natural inclination is to move less\. This fear of movement, sometimes termed *kinesiophobia*, is understandable, but it can set in motion a debilitating downward spiral of deconditioning:

- __Muscle weakness and atrophy:__ Unused muscles quickly lose strength and mass\. Weaker muscles provide less support for joints, which can, in turn, increase pain and instability\.
- __Joint stiffness and reduced range of motion:__ Lack of movement allows joints to stiffen, further restricting mobility and making movement more uncomfortable\.
- __Reduced cardiovascular fitness:__ A sedentary lifestyle leads to a decline in heart and lung health, reducing overall stamina and making even light activity feel taxing\.
- __Weight gain:__ Reduced activity combined with ΓÇ£comfort eatingΓÇ¥ can lead to weight gain, which places additional stress on weight\-bearing joints \(like hips, knees, and the spine\), often exacerbating pain in these areas\.

This deconditioning not only worsens physical symptoms but also erodes independence and quality of life\. Everyday tasks like walking, climbing stairs, grocery shopping, or even personal care can become increasingly difficult, leading to frustration and a shrinking world\. As I often tell my patients, while itΓÇÖs crucial to respect pain, finding safe ways to maintain or gradually increase movement is one of the most important things you can do to combat this decline\.

__Systemic Effects: Impact on Immune Function and Inflammation__

While many chronic pain conditions have a clear inflammatory component \(like RA\), the very experience of persistent pain can act as a chronic stressor that has broader effects on the bodyΓÇÖs systems, including the immune system\. This occurs even in those without an inflammatory disease\.

As mentioned earlier, chronic pain can lead to dysregulation of the HPA axis and the sympathetic nervous system \(our ΓÇ£fight\-or\-flightΓÇ¥ system\)\. Prolonged activation of these stress pathways can contribute to a state of low\-grade systemic inflammation throughout the body\. This isnΓÇÖt the acute, localized inflammation you see with an injury but rather a more subtle, widespread inflammatory state that can have various negative health consequences\. 

*Research update:* There is growing evidence linking the systemic inflammation in chronic pain to an increased risk of other health problems, such as cardiovascular disease and depression\. This finding underscores that chronic pain can have far\-reaching effects on overall physical health\. The interaction between the nervous system and the immune system \(a field called __psychoneuroimmunology__\) is revealing just how intricately pain, stress, and immunity are linked\.

__Changes in Appetite and Weight: A Delicate Balance Disrupted__

The relationship between chronic pain, appetite, and weight is complex and varies greatly from person to person\.

- __Decreased appetite:__ Some people find that persistent pain, nausea \(which can be a symptom of the pain condition or a side effect of medication\), or pain\-related depression leads to a loss of appetite and unintentional weight loss\.
- __Increased appetite and comfort eating:__ For others, pain can trigger an increased desire to eat, particularly foods high in sugar, fat, or salt, as a form of self\-soothing or coping \(comfort eating\)\.
- __Medication effects:__ Many medications used for pain or related symptoms can directly affect appetite or metabolism, leading to either weight gain or loss\. For example, some __antidepressants__ or __anticonvulsants__ are associated with weight gain, while others might suppress appetite\.
- __Impact of reduced activity:__ As discussed, decreased physical activity due to pain often contributes to weight gain if caloric intake isnΓÇÖt adjusted accordingly\.

Significant changes in weight, whether gain or loss, can have their own set of health implications and can also impact pain levels\. Excess weight puts more strain on joints, while significant unintentional weight loss can lead to muscle wasting and weakness\. Finding a healthy nutritional balance becomes even more important when navigating chronic pain\.

The physical burdens of chronic pain extend far beyond the primary sensation of discomfort\. Fatigue, sleep disturbances, deconditioning, and systemic effects on inflammation and metabolism are all part of the challenging dynamic of living with pain\. These issues are not isolated\. They feed into each other, creating complex cycles that can maintain and even worsen the pain experience\. Recognizing these interconnected physical challenges is the first step toward developing strategies to address them, which we will explore as we move through this book\. Next, we will turn our attention to the equally significant emotional and cognitive toll that chronic pain can exact\.

__Chapter 6: The Emotional Storm: Pain, Mood, and Mental Health__

Beyond the direct physical sensations and the systemic consequences weΓÇÖve discussed, chronic pain also affects our emotional and mental well\-being\. The constant presence of pain, the limitations it imposes, and the uncertainty it breeds can stir up turbulent emotions\. In my years of practice and through my own experiences, IΓÇÖve learned that acknowledging and addressing this emotional storm is just as crucial as managing the physical symptoms\. As the biopsychosocial model underscores, the ΓÇ£psychoΓÇ¥ΓÇöour thoughts, emotions, and beliefsΓÇöis not merely a *reaction* to pain but an integral part of the pain experience itself, capable of profoundly influencing its intensity and impact\.

In the original edition of this guide, I touched on the importance of controlling oneΓÇÖs thoughts, sharing worries, and maintaining optimism\. These principles remain vital\. However, our understanding of the intricate dance between pain and mental health has deepened considerably since 2011, revealing shared biological pathways and highlighting the need for integrated treatment approaches\.

__The Pain\-Depression\-Anxiety Triad: A Destructive Alliance__

ItΓÇÖs an unfortunate truth that chronic pain, depression, and anxiety often form a tight, unwelcome trio\. The statistics are clear: People with chronic pain are significantly more likely to experience clinical depression and anxiety disorders than the general population, and vice versa\. This is not only pain causing mood changes\. Mood changes can affect pain as well\. The relationship between mood and pain is bidirectional and mutually reinforcing\.

- __Pain can lead to depression and anxiety:__ Living with constant discomfort, functional limitations, sleep deprivation, social isolation, and uncertainty about the future can understandably trigger feelings of sadness, hopelessness, worry, and fear\. The world can shrink, joy can dim, and a sense of being trapped can become overwhelming\.
- __Depression and anxiety can worsen pain:__ These mental health conditions are not just ΓÇ£in your head\.ΓÇ¥ They involve real, physiological changes that can directly amplify pain\. For example, depression and anxiety can lower the threshold for pain perception, making you more sensitive to discomfort\. They can also deplete neurotransmitters like serotonin and norepinephrine that are involved in the bodyΓÇÖs natural pain\-modulating \(inhibitory\) systems\. Furthermore, the fatigue and lack of motivation associated with depression can lead to inactivity, further worsening pain through deconditioning\.

*Research update:* The neurobiological links between pain, depression, and anxiety are now much clearer\. We know they share overlapping neural circuits, particularly in areas of the brain like the limbic system \(involved in emotion\), the prefrontal cortex \(involved in cognitive control and emotional regulation\), and the insula \(involved in interoception and the emotional aspects of pain\)\. ThereΓÇÖs also compelling evidence that __neuroinflammation__ΓÇöinflammation within the brain and spinal cord, driven by factors like stress, illness, or even signals from the __gut microbiome__ΓÇöplays a crucial role in both chronic pain and mood disorders\. Certain inflammatory cytokines can directly impact neurotransmitter metabolism and neural plasticity in ways that promote both pain sensitization and depressive/anxiety symptoms\. This shared biology means that treatments targeting these common pathways, including certain medications \(like SNRIs\), exercise, and anti\-inflammatory lifestyle strategies, can sometimes help alleviate both pain and mood symptoms simultaneously\.

Recognizing the symptoms is key\. Depression experienced by those with __chronic pain__ isnΓÇÖt always just feeling sad\.
